title: Bialy rolls
subtitle: Jewish-Polish rolls with a caramelised onion centre
excerpt: The Bialystok roll: a yeasted white roll with a dimpled centre filled with caramelised onion and poppy seeds, baked in a hot oven until pale gold. Eaten fresh, without splitting or toasting.
sourceNotes: Bialys originate from Bialystok in north-east Poland and were carried to New York by Jewish immigrants in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The roll is distinguished from the bagel by its baked (not boiled) method and its characteristic onion-filled indent. Early Jewish-American bakery manuals from the 1900s describe the form. The method here follows the classic Bialystok formula as documented in public-domain Polish-Jewish community cookbooks of the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Refresh in a 180°C oven for 5 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be shaped and refrigerated overnight after the first prove. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before filling and baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, high heat. Fan at 210°C.

glossaryTerms.definition: Onions cooked slowly in fat over low heat until the natural sugars caramelise and the onions turn deep golden-brown and sweet. Takes 20 to 30 minutes; rushing with high heat produces soft but not caramelised onions.

content.text: The bialy is related to but distinct from the bagel: no boiling, no shine, a flatter profile, and a small indented well rather than a full hole. The filling of
and poppy seeds goes into the indent before baking, not after. Bialys are best eaten the day they are baked and are not meant for splitting or toasting.

Cook the chopped onions gently in olive oil over low heat for 20 to 25 minutes until soft, golden, and sweet. Do not rush with high heat; the onions need to caramelise, not soften and steam. Season with salt, allow to cool completely, then stir in the poppy seeds. Set aside.
Combine flour, yeast, and salt. Add the water and mix to a rough dough, then knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.

Divide the dough into 8 pieces (about 80 g each). Shape each into a ball, then flatten into a disc about 10 cm across. Place on a lined baking tray, cover, and prove for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 230°C conventional.

Before baking, press a deep indent in the centre of each disc with your fingers and thumbs, pushing the dough outward to form a thin well. Spoon a teaspoon of the onion mixture into each well and spread it to cover the indent. Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until the edges are pale gold and the onion topping is lightly browned. Serve within a few hours of baking.
